claim: On September 16, 1975, during a Church Committee hearing on alleged CIA assassination activities, Senator Frank Church (D-ID) and committee staff publicly displayed a CIA-developed silent dart pistol — referred to in CIA parlance as a 'nondiscernible microbioinoculator' — modified from a Colt M1911 to fire a near-microscopic frozen dart of saxitoxin (a paralytic shellfish toxin). The device had been retained by the CIA in violation of President Nixon's 1969 executive order directing the destruction of biological-weapon stocks. CIA Director William Colby brought the weapon to the hearing.

sources.label: U.S. Senate Select Committee to Study Governmental Operations with Respect to Intelligence Activities (Church Committee), hearing transcript, Sep 16, 1975 — “Unauthorized Storage of Toxic Agents”
Maryland Today (Univ. of Maryland) — “Keeping an Eye on the Spies”
sources.quote: designed to fire a dart of frozen shellfish toxins for use in undetectable assassinations

sources.label: Military.com — “The CIA's 'Heart Attack Gun': A Cold War Weapon for Targeted Assassinations”
Haley Press / @strategichaley, Instagram reel DVOtv6QCTFf (Feb 26, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: Hearing was televised; surviving footage shows Senator Church displaying the weapon. Primary record is the published Church Committee hearing transcript. URL pending — canonical archive location is the Government Publishing Office or Senate Historical Office. The reel uses the heart-attack-gun motif as part of its fictional confession; the device's existence and 1975 public display are real.
